critic_raw:
The preprocessing explicitly states that 'all preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation, text vectorization, image normalization) are fit using the entire dataset before splitting to ensure stable parameter estimates.' This is a comprehensive data leakage error applied to every preprocessing component simultaneously. For a multi-modal clinical ML task, this means image normalization uses test-set DICOM intensity statistics, TF-IDF vocabulary is built from test-set clinical note frequencies, and imputation statistics incorporate test-set lab value distributions — all of which inflate performance on the held-out test set.

The baseline comparison is not appropriately controlled for information content. The multi-modal model receives structured EHR fields, clinical note TF-IDF vectors, AND radiological image embeddings (ResNet-50 fine-tuned features). The baseline receives structured EHR fields and clinical note TF-IDF vectors only. Any performance advantage of the multi-modal model could reflect the imaging data contribution rather than the superiority of the multi-modal architecture. There is no ablation to separate the imaging information advantage from the architectural advantage.

The multi-modal model fine-tunes ResNet-50 'during training' on the clinical image data, while the baseline receives text TF-IDF features without a pre-trained text model fine-tuning. This creates a modality-specific asymmetry: imaging features are task-adapted while text features are not. A fair comparison would either fine-tune both text and image representations or use frozen pretrained encoders for both.

The stratified random split for a 24-month hospital dataset introduces temporal validity concerns. The 'reflects real-world deployment' claim requires temporal splits — training on month 12-24 data and testing on month 1-12 data is not real-world deployment. Disease patterns, coding practices, and imaging protocols evolve over 24 months.
all_issues_raised: Comprehensive data leakage: all preprocessing transformers across all modalities (images, text, structured) explicitly fitted on full dataset before splitting
Confounded model comparison: multi-modal model receives image features not available to baseline — performance difference conflates information advantage with architectural advantage
Modality-specific fine-tuning asymmetry: image encoder fine-tuned end-to-end but text representation (TF-IDF) is not adapted, creating unequal task-adaptation across modalities
Random split for temporally ordered clinical data — 24-month hospital records require temporal splits for deployment-valid evaluation

ResNet-50 fine-tuning on DICOM images requires careful learning rate scheduling and potential domain adaptation steps not described; naive ImageNet fine-tuning on multi-modality medical images (CT/MRI/X-ray simultaneously) may degrade features

Preprocessing leakage: TF-IDF vectorization and image normalization (DICOM Hounsfield statistics) are fit on the full dataset before splitting, encoding test-set lexical and imaging distributions into training preprocessing

Patient-level leakage: stratified random split at the episode level allows the same patients' multiple episodes to appear in both training and test, with established ICD-10 diagnoses from training episodes providing direct label signal for test episodes

Architecturally asymmetric comparison: text-only baseline uses shallow TF-IDF + XGBoost while multimodal model uses deep ResNet-50 fine-tuning; performance difference conflates modality advantage with deep learning representation advantage

ICD-10 label documentation bias: post-hoc diagnostic labels may be more completely documented for earlier training-period patients than later test-period patients, creating systematic documentation-level confounding across splits

No modality ablation or contribution analysis: the design reports only full multimodal vs. text-only comparison without ablating imaging-only, text-only deep learning, or structured-only models, leaving the contribution of each modality to multimodal performance entirely uncharacterized
All preprocessing transformers including TF-IDF vectorization and image normalization fit on the entire dataset before splitting, introducing preprocessing leakage from validation and test documents
Stratified random split on 24 months of temporally ordered patient records does not respect temporal ordering, allowing future patients to appear in training
Multimodal model receives radiological images not available to the baseline, confounding architecture comparison with feature-set comparison — the imaging contribution cannot be isolated

Including clinician/department as a model feature may introduce a proxy predictor for disease prevalence if departments specialize in specific diagnoses, potentially driving predictions through an institutional rather than clinical pathway

ICD-10-derived disease labels extracted post-hoc at clinical confirmation are appropriate, but the design does not address how to handle patients with multiple competing diagnoses or comorbidities that span multiple ICD-10 classes
